column6: message

As some one who’s a avid outdoorsman and music major, I’m the asshole every time except I have loud good sounding music on high quality speakers. Sorry it’s loud, but I don’t really care, that’s my one I don’t give a fuck about. I accommodate for everything except I will never turn off my music. I might turn it down if you ask nicely but be a dick about it and I’ll make sure you leave. I’ve got as much right as you do, and especially since I’m at a campground packed in it’s a party man.

Ok, you seem to believe you have superior taste in music. You cite being a music major, preferring music that is critically acclaimed within its genre, and working as a wedding DJ as evidence for this unstated conclusion. 

As someone with an education background in both classical music and English literature, I admit to feeling that my tastes are better than others’ at times. But I realize projecting that attitude would be extremely off-putting, so I keep that shit to myself. 

You don’t really have much to gain by being “right” about something as subjective as artistic taste. People are going to like whatever they like, regardless of whether or not it’s “good.” Being respectful, open to different perspectives, and humble, though.... those qualities will get you far in life. 

Just my two cents— take it or leave it.

Well shoot, sounds like you think you know better than everyone else. Guess some folks think they're better than others just because they have a degree. Must be nice to think you're so superior. Loud music is loud music, ya know. And campgrounds aren't museums. @average-filet you gotta learn to be a little more considerate.

* Minimal Intervention: Intervention is only permitted for reasons of respect, safety, or process clarification.

* Active Listening: Techniques include nodding, summarizing, and focusing on experiences rather than opinions.

column6:
Because of the electoral college. The top 10 states in the E.C. are as follows:

1. California -- 55 votes--34 Clinton/18 Trump/2 Johnson/1 Stein

2. Texas--38 votes--16 Clinton/21 Trump/1 Johnson

3. New York--29 votes--17 Clinton/11 Trump/1 Johnson

4. Florida-- 29 votes--14 Clinton/15 Trump

5. Illinois--20 votes-- 11 Clinton/8 Trump/1 Johnson

6. Pennsylvania--20 votes-- 10 Clinton/10 Trump

7. Ohio--18 votes-- 8 Clinton/9 Trump/1 Johnson

8. Georgia--16 votes-- 7 Clinton/9 Trump

9. Michigan--16 votes-- 8 Clinton/8 Trump

10. North Carolina--15 votes-- 7 Clinton/8 Trump

So the running total after 10 states is 132 Clinton, 108 Trump, 6 Johnson, 1 Stein, so Clinton is up by 24 votes. In a proportional electoral system, these states should be the best for her because they're the ones that have the most big cities to get a big lead in popular votes. The remaining 40 states plus DC have much smaller populations, and Trump did a lot better.

In most of the remaining states, each state only runs up the number by 1 or 2 for the victor (for example, she won Washington state +18, one of her highest margins, but only got 2 more votes than him. Since he won more states overall, it's not hard to win back that margin.

column6: It seems to me that the multitude of factors that could affect the outcome of any single decision, plus the exponential complexity of compounded choice (i.e. one choice leading to another, and another, etc.) must be contain near-infinite possibilities. What is the difference between an infinitely complex system and true free will? They appear to be the same, and the result is the same, so what does it matter at a fundamental level?

column6:
How do you define "free will"? It seems like you see it as *total* control over our brains, but do you not think there is room for us to exert influence on our choices even inside this context? I think you're right that there is no "pure" free will - i.e. no state of the human mind that exists outside of the influences of our environment/emotions. However I don't think that necessarily makes the opposite (i.e. that *everything* is predetermined, as you've outlined) true.

When an individual exerts willpower to make a change in their life, for example, you believe this was a predetermined thing? Even though their environment and emotional state of mind might have been pushing them in a different direction?

Seriously? You're questioning my entire point about infinite possibilities and free will?! That's incredibly dismissive. I'm trying to make a valid point about the universe's complexity and you're diving into willpower exercises? Get a grip. @pleasant-frequency you missed the mark entirely.

""

Agreed, you're the one that brought it though. I just showed you that even if that was a good analogy, it would still work against you. It's a bad analogy though because no one is born into feminism, so it's even harder to pretend that someone who doesn't see themselves as a feminist could possibly be a feminist.

>I would still argue that Christianity is something one can accidentally be - but I don't think that's something that'll change someones view. 

Once again, religious institutional technicalities aside, genuine faith is a very personal thing that is not predetermined by birth. If someone fundamentally does not agree with or adhere to the religion or culture they were born in, it would be wholly inaccurate to still consider them a part of it.

column6: Pregnancy has no legitimate place in this discussion.  Pregnancy is a choice. If a woman makes that choice she has to live with the ramifications. If a man drinks all night and often comes in late or takes days off to recover from a hangover it's no different. They chose to put themselves in that position. Why should a woman who takes maternity leave or time off for her kids not be penalized for not working as many hours or for taking time off? If person A and person B, genders aside, both start employment in the same job on the same date but person A takes maternity leave for 3 months, they shouldn't expect the same promotion or wage increase as person B who was responsible and didn't miss work for ¼ of a year.

[Here] (http://www.aauw.org/research/the-simple-truth-about-the-gender-pay-gap/) and [here] (http://scholarship.claremont.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1764&context=scripps_theses) are the two sources that I am going to base my argument off of, but some of my views come from an series of briefings that I had to read before going to a session of Harvard Model Congress a few years ago too.

It's evident, as you say, that women work lower paying jobs for less wages. The problem that people have with translating this statistic to mean that women face some form of discrimination is that it does not fully account for the issues that women have as opposed to men with working. I read an interesting article on Breitbart by Milo once [about capping women's acceptance rates](http://www.breitbart.com/big-government/2015/06/15/heres-why-there-ought-to-be-a-cap-on-women-studying-science-and-maths/) in STEM fields at "5-10%" that I think suffers from this same lack of understanding (if you read this article, keep in mind that he effectively picks every single statistic that could support his position and ignores a huge amount of evidence that suggests that its not that "women can't hack it").

The fact of the matter is that women can get pregnant. Once pregnancy begins, women in high intensity fields, like pilots, doctors, and professors, face a serious setback. These fields are centered around stressful and demanding schedules, get harder as one gets older, and often (in academia and healthcare particularly) employ tiered heirachies for pay grades and qualifications. These fields are NOT something that you would want to get behind in, and they certainly do not benefit from needing to take off time to raise a child. Additionally, the US has no mandatory maternity leave on the federal level. Not only are women often faced with short maternity leaves that fail to monetarily provide for them in a necessary way, they are faced with partners who cannot assume their role as a constant parent (with their own paternity leave [also think about how terrible this would be as a father, not being able to connect to your child when they're first born because you have to go to your middle level advertising job]) when the leave ends, sometimes after as short or shorter than 3 months. Imagine you are a women with a three month old infant, who is faced with the choice of leaving her baby to another so she can resume her PhD defense while her husband works a cooperate job or staying home and being a full time mother. Many women choose to opt for motherhood here. 

This is even on top of the fact that there is, as you acknowledge, institutionalized discrimination. Women are proven to be less likely to pursue higher paying jobs in ambitious and stressful degrees, something that is changing because it is likely a symptom of society telling them what they should and shouldn't do as they grow up. These two things, institutionalized discrimination and inadequate maternity leave, are a one-two sucker punch that is incredibly hard for women to overcome, especially on top of whatever real discrimination exists.

Hopefully this answer is enough. I didn't want to type a full essay on reddit but I'd be happy to try and answer any additional questions that you may have.

column6:
This is why I have mixed feelings about weapon free zones, because if someone brings a weapon in everyone is defenseless. Obviously I'd rather he had a knife, but he can still buy an AK-47 illegally if he wants to. Either way I am left defenseless. 

There are simply too many guns in the United States to just make them disappear. I live in Canada where we have stricter gun control and criminals still get handguns easily. There have been a lot of shootings recently with guns that are illegal here.

Ideally we would have a world with no weapons at all, but we've been killing people since the start of humanity, I don't see it changing in the future.

The attempts may not go down but the results would certainly change, guns are a force multiplier that a knife never will be. Bombs are much more difficult to use to equal effect,  they also require more expertise and preparation. There is no better way to kill a lot of people quickly than guns. Be honest if you were sitting in school and a person came in with the intent to kill as many people as possible would you rather he had a knife or an AK-47?

column6:
> Of course it does. Same as Christianity has something to do with it if they were killed in a Christian country like Uganda. The two religions are extraordinarily similar in structure anyway.

Thats... exactly what i'm trying to say to you. If you understand that why are you singling out Islam?

> But you could also argue, 'If alcohol causes people to do bad things, then how come my neighbor who has a few drinks at the BBQ doesn't beat his kids or get in car crashes?' Alcohol is inherently a poison. It unarguably alters perceptions and inhibitions. But whether or not you do bad things while under its influence depends very much on how much you've put into you, and what kind of person you are at your core

This is exactly my point...

Would you ban alcohol because of the damage it does to society?

> I did point out four separate surveys where a majority of Muslims in a whole lot of countries all think homosexuality is immoral. 

But people in non-Islamic countries think the same thing as well.

So what does Islam have to do with anything? Why are you against Islam and not just against homophobia?
column18: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column6:
>But Islam has nothing to do with it if they could have been killed for being gay in a Christian nation, does it? 

Of course it does. Same as Christianity has something to do with it if they were killed in a Christian country like Uganda. The two religions are *extraordinarily similar in structure* anyway.

>Which countries? If you're asking about the Islamic countries where homosexuality is illegal then now, obviously they are not living peacefully.

Thank you.

>If Islam causes people do to bad things, why is my neighbour Islamic and a totally solid dude who doesnt hate gay people or behead any one?

(Serious question: have you ASKED if he thinks homosexuality is morally wrong?)

I'm guessing you and your neighbors are American. If so, then I'd say it has to do with culture. In cultures where open homophobia is permissible, more people will be openly homophobic. And vice versa.

But you could also argue, 'If alcohol causes people to do bad things, then how come my neighbor who has a few drinks at the BBQ doesn't beat his kids or get in car crashes?' Alcohol is inherently a poison. It unarguably alters perceptions and inhibitions. But whether or not you do bad things while under its influence depends very much on **how much you've put into you, and what kind of person you are at your core**. Most American Muslims I've seen are the same as American Christians: they follow a version of their religion that's completely watered-down, and they adhere to government laws more than religious ones.

>If Islam is driving the behavior that you and I both despise, why arent all Islamic people that way?

It doesn't have to be ALL or NONE, you know. I did point out four separate surveys where a majority of Muslims in a whole lot of countries all think homosexuality is immoral. That kinda sinks your point here.

Whoa hold on a sec. This is getting kinda heated lol. Seriously tho, gotta admit, some of these arguments are kinda missing the mark. @loud-manor you gotta think about context, ya know? Like, saying one religion is better than another is kinda whack. @stern-cod, chill out dude. Just cause someone believes something doesn't mean they're automatically bad. Gotta be respectful, ya know? And @loud-manor, surveys are cool but they don't tell the whole story. People are complicated.

* Active Listening: Techniques include nodding, summarizing, and focusing on experiences rather than opinions.

{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### The Role of the Moderator

* Environment and Care: Moderators create a warm environment, treating participants as human beings first.

* Process Management: Duties include timekeeping, monitoring energy levels, and managing physical needs like bio breaks or stretching.

* Participation Balancing: Mods must encourage shared "air time," normalize silence for thinking, and ensure participants talk to each other rather than through the moderator.

* Conflict and Behavior: The role involves highlighting points of disagreement to reach understanding and interrupting problematic behaviors like microaggressions or personal slights.

### Limitations and Boundaries ("What NOT to do")

* Non-Directive Approach: Moderators are not decision-makers; they act as "support staff" or "tour managers" rather than conductors.

* Deferring to participants: Legitimacy lies with the participants, who set their own agenda and write/edit all content.

* Content Neutrality: Mods must not use their own knowledge of sustainability or fact-check participants, maintaining a strict separation between process and content.

* Minimal Intervention: Intervention is only permitted for reasons of respect, safety, or process clarification.

### Tools for Effective Facilitation
* Active Listening: Techniques include nodding, summarizing, and focusing on experiences rather than opinions.

* Non-Content Prompts: Helpful phrases include "Tell me more," "What is the story behind that?", and "Who hasn’t had a chance to weigh in?".

* Bias Awareness: Moderators are encouraged to identify subconscious biases toward activists or administrators and to intentionally engage with ideas they may instinctively dislike.

column6:
It's not different with women.  I even stated in my initial post that a woman doing this makes me uncomfortable.  This is my body.  It is not your body.  You do not have the right to subject me to unwanted physical contact without my consent.  The gender is irrelevant (even beyond me, you don't know always know the sexual orientation of the person you are hugging).  

How do you realistically telegraph that you want to hug someone?  I think it's unreasonable that you're asking me that, but you expect someone to use body language to communicate that they do NOT want a hug.  If you don't know a person well enough to use your body language to clearly communicate your desire to hug them, then as I originally posted, you should ASK.  Use your words.  "Are you comfortable with me giving you a hug?" or some variation of that statement.

You are not incapable of obtaining consent for a hug.  Your desire to hug someone does not justify violating another person's body.

I'm not a hugger, so my eye is already twitching.  /u/domino_stars raises an excellent point about people who have already experienced trauma from non-consensual touch can and do freeze and are unable to express their verbal non-consent.  So I'll address the "it's not a huge deal".

First, ever culture has their own unspoken rules regarding physical contact.  I was raised in a touch-conservative family and in a touch-conservative culture (certain region of America where hugging people who aren't close to you isn't done).  I expect a certain amount of personal space in a public area and especially between strangers.  I do not want, nor do I expect, a stranger to be comfortable hugging me.  I am a petite build.  If a larger man or woman comes in and violates my personal space - and it is a violation - that is a rattling experience because I am not used to it, it is not culturally appropriate, and my body is not yours to treat however you want.

By touching my body without my explicit consent, you have violated my trust in you (which as someone new was minimal to begin with), you have revealed that you do not care enough about my self agency to not be touched which is threatening and you have let me know that you do not care that this makes me uncomfortable which telegraphs a certain sense of self-superiority on your part.  Your desire to violate my body is more important than my right to not be touched against my will.

Will I wind up weeping in the corner?  No.  But my level of anxiety will be increased for quite some time and it's definitely going to color not only any future interactions with you, but with whoever was stupid enough to introduce me to the kind of person that you are.

Body language is interpreted by different people in different ways and is definitely not consent for another person to touch you.  A person who wants to hug another is more likely to misinterpret signals or, again disrespect the individual who does not want to be hugged.

Your desire to hug does is not important enough to violate another person's body.  If you want a hug, you can use body language to communicate that you want to engage in a hug without touching the person, thus respecting the person and their body enough to let them choose if they want to engage in the hug.  Or you can just ask them.  The onus shouldn't be on the person you wish to hug.

Okay, wow. This is getting intense. Seriously questioning everything now. @regular-filter, I guess I didn't fully grasp the nuance in my initial post. I guess I didn't think it was a huge deal, but @buoyant-grenade is hitting on something really important here about consent and boundaries. It's not about comfort levels, it's about respect. Gonna think about this more.

column6:
>The Bible is the word of God, which we know because God tells us this in the Bible.

That's not a fallacy.  "My research paper is my word, and we know this because I tell you so in my research paper".  Or, "My comment is the word of me, which we know because I tell you this in my comment."  It's mostly a statement of ipso facto.  The Word of God is true, the Bible spreads the Word of God (meaning his message; rarely does God speak directly in the Bible, and even then it's through someone like Moses or Jesus).  Therefore the Bible is the Word of God.

I'm not a practicing Catholic or religious at all.  One can easily find this to be silly or dumb or okay or kind of a good idea maybe, but that doesn't mean we need to paint it like some logical fallacy - especially when it's not asserting this premise at all.

God is an omnipotent being and all-knowing.  What he says is true regardless of a book's existence.  The Bible is a way for Christians to keep the Word of God alive and spread its message, as it was done for hundreds and thousands of years.

But the Bible isn't true due to circular reasoning.  That's something bad comedians and bitter agnostics come up with to make a joke.  Christian thinking here is very simple and straightforward, and one can easily disagree with many of its teachings, but to make a circular argument out of nothing is pointless.

When God spoke to Abraham, he didn't need a Bible.  He was God, and could perform things no one could.  Jesus was the same way.  The Bible was mankind's attempt to spread the Word of God, not be the only words of God ever.  Catholics still honor saints, showing that they believe miracles are being performed.  They're just very picky about them.  Non-Catholics continually pray to God and see events as being signs of his existence.  These can also be the actions or words of God.

To Christians, God has already demonstrated his power.  It would stand to reason that what he says is True with a capital T.  But if God came back to Earth and said some stuff in the Bible was no longer applicable, it would be done with.

Seriously?! This is getting ridiculous. People are twisting scripture to fit their own agendas. It's not about logic here, it's about faith. And frankly, some of these explanations are just plain lazy. @plum-death, you need to take a step back and think about what you're saying. It feels incredibly dismissive to millions who find meaning in the Bible.

column6:
> Feminists and SJW also call for witch hunts against basically all males

This is just a blanket unsupported claim. Please cite the actual feminists who are not teenagers who call for witch hunts. OP's thesis is that the so-called social justice warriors have no real power of influence. Please cite the feminist leaders, not inconsequential nobody's on Tumblr, who say these things.

> Then you have people like Anita Sarkeesian and Zoe Quinn wasting the time of the UN to try and convince them to essentially make disagreeing with women on the internet a criminal offense as it is "cyber violence".

You have a tendency to state highly biased claims as if they were facts. Anita Sarkeesian and many other women online have been subjected to truly horrific levels of hate speech and vitriol. THey are indeed examples of violence and absolutely something should be done about it.

> The same can be said to the BLM movement.

And there we have it. The racism, white supremacy, along side of the anti woman position.

> Even on reddit there are ideology driven mods that will ban you from multiple sub reddits for expressing the wrong opinion.

Reddit is not the internet. You have NO RIGHT to post to any particular forum on Reddit. None at all. Forum mods in Reddit have the right to set the conditions for posting any way they choose. As a liberal democrat I cannot post my political views in /r/conservative. You don't hear me whining like a child over it. I recognize that the mods there have the right to moderate their forum according to whatever standards they please.

> And speaking of schools... in some countries the "male teacher" is kind of going extinct with less and less young males deciding to pursue a career in teaching

Again, unsupported claims. Please cite *evidence* to support your claims.

>  there are parents who force their children to be a part of their ideology

They have that right. All parents have the right to raise their children in the religious or political ideology of their choosing.

>  One more that just jumped into my mind is the whole concept of "cultural appropiation" which also aims to basically police everything about individuals right down to what hairstyle they have, what food they are allowed to eat, what music they are allowed to listen to, what clothes they wear and so on.

Here you are again conflating two things. Cultural appropriation is a real thing. It is what Elvis Presley did. He appropriated black entertainers like Little Richard to make them acceptable to the white racist general audience of the US at that time. No one is telling people what hairstyles they can wear. No one. This is just MMA nonsense conspiracy theories you've dreamt up somewhere.

column6:
It's true that luckily many of the more extreme elements of these ideologies are not taken that serious by most people for now.

However I do think that ignoring the creeping influence and potential harm it can cause isn't that healthy, though it might be okay in Canada for now and the foreseeable future.

But there are already real concerns to be had.

Just think back to the whole "ban bossy" campaign for example which was a real attempt (as ludicrous as it was) to police language being used, even if it wouldn't have affected anyone outside the US.

Then you have people like Anita Sarkeesian and Zoe Quinn wasting the time of the UN to try and convince them to essentially make disagreeing with women on the internet a criminal offense as it is "cyber violence".

On american colleges and universities you have a comeback of racial segregation which is now in newspeak "safe spaces" and this time mostly aim to exclude people of white skin color. The same is true to unofficial arbitrary "free-speech" or "journalist-free" zones, going as far as to threaten people with violence to get their way.

The media themselves usually side with the feminist and sjw narrative because a good victim story brings in the views and what better than to put a young (debatably) attractive distressed damsel in the spotlight who is bullied by a bunch of mean basement dwelling women hating neckbeards who are essentially terrorists? 

Events aiming to address issues facing men are also regularily protested or shut down by feminist groups by pulling fire alarms, blocking entrance, harrassing listeners or being otherwise a disrupting force.

The same can be said to the BLM movement.

Feminists and SJW also call for witch hunts against basically all males by increasingly demanding that all rape accusation are true and that the accused is to be treated guilty until proven innocent and if he turns out to be innocents then it's a failure of the justice system. 

Then there's the blatant and very worrying amount of censorship pretty much everywhere you look, especially on the internet but also on tv, though it depends on where you live. Even on reddit there are ideology driven mods that will ban you from multiple sub reddits for expressing the wrong opinion. Twitter has a huge double standard when it comes to who is allowed on their platform and who isn't and literally has a council of members who decide what goes and what doesn't, mostly to support certain ideologies.

You even have high profile politicians pander to these ideologies by having people like Obama mentioning the wage gap myth or you have Hillary Clinton who, when all else fails, can still pull the gender card and tell people with a straight face than her being a woman is an advantage in her favour.

Plus not all politicians need to be high-profile to have a negative influence.

As mentioned Canada might be doing okay for now, but take Germany for example. It is a country that could very well collapse under its need to appear politically correct by taking in more refugees than it can actually handle. But what is even worse is that these refugees are almost a protected class at the moment with both politicians and the police themselves suppressing crime stories about the wrongdoings of refugees, sometimes going as far as to blame the victims even if they are children. There's also what essentially amounts to a new form of the Stasi which is called the Antonio Foundation which surveys german social media for signs of wrong think and report it to the goverment so a team of police officer can bust in some doors.

Hell there're even more and more german schools that decide to not offer any more pork because it could offend some students.

And speaking of schools... in some countries the "male teacher" is kind of going extinct with less and less young males deciding to pursue a career in teaching, part of the reason being the unwelcoming and toxic enviroment created by these ideologies and the great risk you take with the job as a single accusation from either a co-worker or a student could end your career.

In the short or long run this would leave impressionable children to the whims of teachers who are eager to push a certain ideology onto them and these kind of teachers do exist.

And speaking of children... these are fringe cases of course, but there are parents who force their children to be a part of their ideology, though that isn't exclusive to feminist or SJW types.

As a (former) viewer of Sargon and Co. you might have even heard of many of the things I've just mentioned.

My point simply is that despite numbers and usually being ridiculed... these ideologies can and do have influence and that influence, like any other, should be kept in check. Some of the examples I have raised did not come to pass, while others either are or will be a reality.

I do hope I somehow managed to hit on at least a few points you have raised.

Edit: One more that just jumped into my mind is the whole concept of "cultural appropiation" which also aims to basically police everything about individuals right down to what hairstyle they have, what food they are allowed to eat, what music they are allowed to listen to, what clothes they wear and so on.

Edit: OP has removed his post (or it was removed by someone else?) so this all has kind of lost relevance.

Edit: Also thanks to everyone who did directly or indirectly supported me and even to the people opposed for presenting a different point of view and having some discussion.

Wow, a lot to unpack here. It sounds like you're really concerned about a lot of things. It's interesting to see different perspectives on these issues. I guess it's tough to navigate these conversations sometimes. @direct-bagel, I guess it's good to be aware of different viewpoints, even if they aren't always aligned with your own.

column6:
It's mainly because it's a "slippery slope." If they can collect your browsing habits on Facebook and sell it to advertisers, why can't Facebook & Co. sell that information to:

- Political entities (ie legislatures) seeking to gerrymander or suppress voters with views that make them unlikely to vote for them

- Employers, who could see if you clicked on a story titled "Tips for switching careers" and you are fired or passed over for a raise or promotion as a result

- Insurers, who could deny you coverage or raise your premiums if you searched for "Signs of cancer" or spent a lot of time on WebMD.

When information becomes public, people can get hurt.

Being illegal doesn't stop companies from doing it. Insurance companies denied coverage by genetic testing as recently as 2001, and gerrymandering is a big problem in many US states. They have the means and the history.
